title: Binary Trees
description: A Node with TWO children instead of one next -- branching structure built manually, just like the Linked List module's Node class.
objectives: Understand a binary tree Node holding left and right children
Build a small binary tree manually and understand its terminology

sections.text: 1. From One Next to Two Children

In Singly Linked List, each Node had exactly one next reference, forming a straight chain. A binary tree's Node has TWO references instead -- left and right -- letting the structure BRANCH rather than just chain.
A hierarchical structure where each Node has at most two children, conventionally called left and right, starting from a single root node.
2. The Node Class and Tree Terminology

sections.title: Tree vocabulary
rows: The single topmost node -- every other node is reached by descending from it
A node with no children (both left and right are null)
The number of edges on the longest path from root to a leaf
The number of edges from the root to that specific node
Any node treated as the root of its own smaller tree, formed by it and its descendants

sections.title: Family tree analogy
sections.text: Think of an org chart: one CEO (root) at the top, each manager having at most two direct reports (left and right), branching downward. Someone with no reports is a leaf. This is exactly a binary tree's shape, just capped at two children per node.
3. Why Recursion Fits Trees So Naturally

Recall Recursion Basics: a recursive function needs a base case and a recursive case that shrinks toward it. A tree is naturally recursive -- its left subtree and right subtree are themselves complete, smaller binary trees. Most tree algorithms are written by handling the current node, then recursing into left and right.

sections.title: Tracing height(root) where root=10, root.left=5, root.right=15, root.left.left=2

sections.text: Returning -1 for an empty (null) tree is a common convention that makes the +1 arithmetic work out cleanly -- a single node (no children) then correctly computes height 0, not height 1. Some definitions instead treat a single node's height as 1 -- always check which convention a specific problem expects.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting the base case (node == null) in recursive tree functions -- this causes a NullPointerException the moment you try node.left on a null node.
Confusing height (longest path to a LEAF) with depth (path from the ROOT to a specific node) -- they're measured in opposite directions.
Assuming every binary tree is sorted or balanced -- a plain binary tree makes no such guarantee; that's specifically what the Binary Search Tree lesson adds.
Writing iterative code for tree problems by instinct, when the recursive version is often far simpler given a tree's naturally recursive structure.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is a binary tree?
questions.answer: A hierarchical structure where each node has at most two children (left and right), starting from a single root node -- unlike a linked list's single next reference, a tree branches.

questions.question: What is the difference between a tree's height and a specific node's depth?
questions.answer: Height is the number of edges on the longest path from the root down to a leaf. Depth is the number of edges from the root down to that specific node -- height looks at the whole tree's longest path, depth looks at one node's distance from the top.

questions.question: Why do most tree algorithms use recursion naturally?
questions.answer: A tree's left and right subtrees are themselves complete, smaller binary trees -- this self-similar structure fits recursion's pattern of solving a problem by relying on smaller versions of itself, with the null-child case serving as the base case.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A binary tree's Node holds up to two children, left and right, rooted at a single starting node -- unlike a linked list's single next reference, this lets the structure branch. Key terms: root (topmost node), leaf (no children), height (longest root-to-leaf path in edges), and depth (a specific node's distance from the root). Trees are naturally recursive, since each subtree is itself a complete smaller tree -- most tree functions handle the current node then recurse into left and right, with a null-node base case, exactly as shown by computing height recursively.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Binary tree = an org chart capped at 2 direct reports per manager. Height = longest chain of command to the newest hire (a leaf). Every subtree is itself a smaller org chart -- that's why recursion fits so naturally.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: What two references does a binary tree's Node hold?
What is the difference between height and depth?
Why does recursion fit tree problems so naturally?
